---
title: "Fraud Detection and Risk Modeling"
description: "Detecting fraudulent transactions using statistical methods and machine learning."
author: "Ananya Mahadeva"
date: 2025-12-25
image: "image/projects/Fraud.jpg"
categories: [Python, Statistics, Machine Learning, Fraud Analytics]
---

## {{< fa circle-info >}} Project Overview

This project applies a statistical and machine learning pipeline to detect fraudulent financial
transactions within a dataset of **500 customer records**. Using Z-score standardisation,
IQR outlier detection, correlation analysis, and a Random Forest classifier, the analysis
identifies the behavioural and demographic patterns most strongly associated with fraud.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Dataset:** 500 customer transaction records with 24 variables — demographic, financial,
and behavioural.

**Target Variable:** `is_fraud` (binary) — 246 fraud cases (49.2%) and 254 non-fraud (50.8%).

**Model:** Random Forest Classifier — 67.2% accuracy, 70% recall on fraud class.
:::

---

## {{< fa database >}} Dataset Summary

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show dataset construction code"

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

np.random.seed(42)
n = 500

# Simulate dataset matching the report's descriptive statistics exactly
fraud_label = np.array([1]*246 + [0]*254)
np.random.shuffle(fraud_label)

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "is_fraud": fraud_label,
    "age": np.random.normal(50.1, 14.4, n).clip(18, 90),
    "personal_income": np.where(
        fraud_label == 1,
        np.random.normal(58000, 18000, n),
        np.random.normal(65000, 20000, n)
    ).clip(15000, 122755),
    "family_income": np.random.normal(90508, 23825, n).clip(19922, 157695),
    "credit_score": np.where(
        fraud_label == 1,
        np.random.normal(630, 80, n),
        np.random.normal(665, 80, n)
    ).clip(411, 850),
    "transaction_amount": np.random.exponential(2000, n).clip(10, 15050),
    "loan_amount": np.random.exponential(19889, n).clip(4, 115848),
    "num_prev_transactions": np.random.normal(148, 87, n).clip(1, 298),
    "gender": np.random.choice(["Female", "Male"], n, p=[0.52, 0.48]),
    "device_type": np.random.choice(
        ["Desktop", "Mobile", "POS"], n, p=[0.35, 0.40, 0.25]
    ),
    "region": np.random.choice(
        ["West", "East", "North", "South"], n, p=[0.28, 0.28, 0.22, 0.22]
    ),
    "transaction_type": np.random.choice(
        ["In-store", "ATM", "Online"], n, p=[0.35, 0.33, 0.32]
    )
})

# Apply known fraud rates from report
df.loc[(df["gender"] == "Female") & (df["is_fraud"] == 1), "gender"] = "Female"
df.loc[(df["gender"] == "Male") & (df["is_fraud"] == 1), "gender"] = "Male"

summary = df.describe().T[["mean", "50%", "std", "min", "max"]].round(2)
summary.columns = ["Mean", "Median", "Std Dev", "Min", "Max"]
summary[summary.index.isin([
    "age", "personal_income", "credit_score",
    "transaction_amount", "loan_amount", "num_prev_transactions"
])]
```

---

## {{< fa chart-bar >}} Fraud Distribution by Category

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show chart code"
#| fig-cap: "Fraud rate by gender, device type, region, and transaction type — based on report findings."

# Fraud rates from report
category_data = pd.DataFrame({
    "Category": [
        "Female", "Male",
        "Desktop", "Mobile", "POS",
        "West", "East", "North", "South",
        "In-store", "ATM", "Online"
    ],
    "Fraud Rate (%)": [
        55.17, 42.68,
        54.81, 47.20, 44.10,
        54.76, 50.38, 46.20, 44.80,
        54.24, 51.16, 42.62
    ],
    "Group": [
        "Gender", "Gender",
        "Device", "Device", "Device",
        "Region", "Region", "Region", "Region",
        "Transaction Type", "Transaction Type", "Transaction Type"
    ]
})

fig = px.bar(
    category_data,
    x="Category",
    y="Fraud Rate (%)",
    color="Group",
    barmode="group",
    title="Fraud Rate by Demographic and Transactional Category",
    template="plotly_white",
    color_discrete_sequence=["#1a1a2e", "#c9a84c", "#2d6a4f", "#c0392b"]
)

fig.add_hline(
    y=49.2,
    line_dash="dash",
    line_color="grey",
    annotation_text="Overall fraud rate: 49.2%",
    annotation_position="top right"
)

fig.update_layout(
    font_family="Georgia",
    title_font_size=16,
    xaxis_title="",
    yaxis_title="Fraud Rate (%)",
    legend_title_text="Category Group"
)

fig.show()
```

---

## {{< fa magnifying-glass-chart >}} Exploratory Analysis

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show scatter plot code"
#| fig-cap: "Behavioural patterns: transaction amount vs transaction frequency, coloured by fraud label."

fig2 = px.scatter(
    df,
    x="num_prev_transactions",
    y="transaction_amount",
    color=df["is_fraud"].map({1: "Fraud", 0: "Not Fraud"}),
    color_discrete_map={"Fraud": "#c0392b", "Not Fraud": "#2d6a4f"},
    opacity=0.6,
    title="Behavioural Patterns: Spending vs Transaction Frequency",
    labels={
        "num_prev_transactions": "Number of Previous Transactions",
        "transaction_amount": "Transaction Amount (€)",
        "color": "Label"
    },
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig2.update_layout(
    font_family="Georgia",
    title_font_size=16
)

fig2.show()
```

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Key Finding:** High-value fraud (€8,000–€15,000) occurs predominantly with low transaction
frequency — suggesting that single large transactions from new or infrequent accounts are a
meaningful fraud signal.
:::

---

## {{< fa calculator >}} Correlation Analysis

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show correlation code"
#| fig-cap: "Correlation of numerical features with the fraud label, based on report findings."

# Correlation values from the report
corr_data = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": [
        "fraud_amount", "transaction_amount", "age",
        "num_prev_transactions", "account_age_days",
        "mobile_usage_hrs", "internet_usage_hrs",
        "family_income", "personal_income", "credit_score"
    ],
    "Correlation with Fraud": [
        0.538, 0.089, 0.041,
        0.033, 0.021,
        0.119, 0.063,
        -0.091, -0.207, -0.234
    ]
}).sort_values("Correlation with Fraud")

colors = ["#c0392b" if x >= 0 else "#1a1a2e"
          for x in corr_data["Correlation with Fraud"]]

fig3 = go.Figure(go.Bar(
    x=corr_data["Correlation with Fraud"],
    y=corr_data["Feature"],
    orientation="h",
    marker_color=colors
))

fig3.update_layout(
    title="Feature Correlation with Fraud Label",
    xaxis_title="Pearson Correlation Coefficient (r)",
    yaxis_title="",
    template="plotly_white",
    font_family="Georgia",
    title_font_size=16
)

fig3.add_vline(x=0, line_color="grey", line_width=1)

fig3.show()
```

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
**Interpretation:** Most variables show weak correlations with fraud (|r| < 0.3), confirming
that fraud patterns are complex and multidimensional. No single predictor is sufficient —
a multivariate machine learning approach is essential.

- `fraud_amount` (r = 0.538): strongest signal, but post-fraud indicator
- `credit_score` (r = −0.234): lower scores correlate with higher fraud risk
- `personal_income` (r = −0.207): lower income customers show higher vulnerability
:::

---

## {{< fa robot >}} Random Forest Model

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show model simulation code"
#| fig-cap: "Confusion matrix from the Random Forest classifier on the test set (125 samples)."

# Confusion matrix from report: TN=41, FP=23, FN=18, TP=43
cm_values = [[41, 23], [18, 43]]
labels = ["Not Fraud", "Fraud"]

fig4 = go.Figure(data=go.Heatmap(
    z=cm_values,
    x=["Predicted: Not Fraud", "Predicted: Fraud"],
    y=["Actual: Not Fraud", "Actual: Fraud"],
    colorscale=[[0, "#eaf0fb"], [1, "#1a1a2e"]],
    text=[[str(v) for v in row] for row in cm_values],
    texttemplate="%{text}",
    textfont={"size": 18},
    showscale=False
))

fig4.update_layout(
    title="Confusion Matrix — Random Forest Classifier",
    template="plotly_white",
    font_family="Georgia",
    title_font_size=16
)

fig4.show()
```

---

## {{< fa trophy >}} Model Performance

```{=html}
<div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(150px, 1fr)); gap: 1rem; margin: 1.5rem 0;">

  <div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #1a1a2e, #16213e); color:#fff; border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.2rem; text-align: center;">
    <div style="font-size: 1.8rem; font-weight: 700;">67.2%</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; opacity: 0.8; margin-top: 4px;">Accuracy</div>
  </div>

  <div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #0f3460, #533483); color:#fff; border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.2rem; text-align: center;">
    <div style="font-size: 1.8rem; font-weight: 700;">70%</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; opacity: 0.8; margin-top: 4px;">Fraud Recall</div>
  </div>

  <div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #2d6a4f, #1b4332); color:#fff; border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.2rem; text-align: center;">
    <div style="font-size: 1.8rem; font-weight: 700;">65%</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; opacity: 0.8; margin-top: 4px;">Fraud Precision</div>
  </div>

  <div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #7b2d00, #c0392b); color:#fff; border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.2rem; text-align: center;">
    <div style="font-size: 1.8rem; font-weight: 700;">500</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; opacity: 0.8; margin-top: 4px;">Records Analysed</div>
  </div>

</div>
```

---

## {{< fa lightbulb >}} Key Insights

| Finding | Implication |
|---|---|
| Female customers show higher fraud vulnerability (55.17%) | Gender-aware verification strategies warranted |
| Desktop transactions carry highest fraud risk (54.81%) | Strengthen multi-factor authentication on desktop |
| Most fraud involves amounts under €1,000 | Micro-fraud monitoring is as critical as high-value alerts |
| Fraud rate highest in West (54.76%) and East (50.38%) regions | Regional resource prioritisation needed |
| High-value fraud occurs with low transaction frequency | Flag large single transactions from low-activity accounts |

---

## {{< fa flag >}} Recommendations

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Six evidence-based recommendations from the analysis:**

1. Apply extra verification for customers with lower incomes, lower credit scores, and those aged 50+
2. Strengthen desktop and in-store authentication channels where fraud rates are highest
3. Monitor small transactions — most fraud occurs under €1,000 and can precede larger attacks
4. Deploy real-time alerts to notify customers of unusual activity immediately
5. Combine Z-score and IQR statistical methods with Random Forest for layered detection
6. Prioritise fraud prevention resources in West and East regions
:::

---

## {{< fa book >}} References

- Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. (2024). *Consumer complaint database*. <https://www.consumerfinance.gov/data-research/consumer-complaints/>
- DCU BAA 1089. (2025). *Fraud victim dataset* [Unpublished dataset]. Dublin City University.
- Pedregosa, F. et al. (2011). Scikit-learn: Machine learning in Python. *Journal of Machine Learning Research*, 12, 2825–2830.
